title: Tetrapilus wightianus
taxonomy: Tetrapilus wightianus is a species of plant in the family Anacardiaceae. It belongs to the kingdom Plantae and the class Magnoliopsida. The order and family of Tetrapilus wightianus are Sapindales and Anacardiaceae respectively. Tetrapilus wightianus is a type of tree that is native to certain regions. Further classification details may be found in botanical sources for more precise information.
uses: Tetrapilus wightianus has been used in traditional Ayurvedic medicine for its potential health benefits. The plant is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help in managing conditions such as fever and digestive issues. In addition, it is also used to treat various skin problems, including eczema and acne. The plant's leaves and roots are typically used in herbal remedies. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Tetrapilus wightianus as a medicinal treatment.

intro: Tetrapilus wightianus, also known as Indian calotropis or the Devil's snare, is a poisonous plant species native to the tropical regions of India and Southeast Asia. Traditionally, the plant has been used in Ayurvedic medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin infections, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Tetrapilus wightianus include alkaloids, glycosides, and flavonoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, the plant is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, or used in the form of a paste or ointment to treat various ailments.
